text: From those to whom much is given, much is expected.
context: The moral center of the speech on using success to solve global inequity.

text: Humanity's greatest advances are not in its discoveries, but in how those discoveries are applied to reduce inequity.
context: Call to creative capitalism and applying technology and wealth to the poorest.

text: We are all created equal in the eyes of God, but we are not equal in the eyes of man or in the eyes of the market.
context: Highlighting the injustice that market forces alone won't solve for the bottom billion.

text: If you believe that every life has equal value, it's revolting to learn that some lives are seen as worth saving and others are not.
context: On the moral imperative to apply innovation to global health disparities.

text: The world is getting better, but it's not getting better fast enough, and it's not getting better for everyone.
context: Urging graduates to use their privilege to accelerate progress for the poorest.

text: Creative capitalism is about making the market work for the poor as well as the rich.
source: World Economic Forum and Harvard speech
context: Insightful framework for using business incentives to solve social problems.

text: We need to make the market work for the poorest people in the world.
context: Core idea that innovation and markets can be harnessed for equity.

text: Optimism is a moral choice. Pessimism is a moral failure.
context: Encouragement to focus on solvable problems rather than despair.

text: The best way to predict the future is to create it — but only if we apply our talents to the right problems.
context: Linking personal success to responsibility for reducing suffering.

text: We have to find a way to make the breakthroughs that will allow the poorest people to live better lives.
context: Call for the privileged to direct energy toward the most pressing inequities.
